questions.question:
Complete the sentence:

Universalism is to particularism as diffuseness is to _______________.

A. specificity  B. neutrality  C. generality  D. adaptation
questions.to_find: the word that completes the analogy: diffuseness is to ___ as universalism is to particularism

understand.plain: This is a word analogy question. Universalism and particularism are antonyms — universalism applies rules broadly to everyone while particularism applies them narrowly based on specific relationships. Similarly, diffuseness (breadth, vagueness, spreading widely) must be paired with its antonym, which describes something narrow and specific. The answer must mirror the same oppositional relationship.

keywords.explain: The principle of applying the same rules and standards to all people regardless of personal relationships.
keywords.example: A judge who treats every defendant equally regardless of who they are is practicing universalism.

keywords.explain: The principle of applying different rules based on individual relationships or specific contexts — the opposite of universalism.
keywords.example: A manager who gives preferential treatment to close friends is practicing particularism.

keywords.explain: The quality of being spread out, vague, or broadly scoped rather than focused; covering many things loosely.
keywords.example: A diffuse job description covers many vague responsibilities rather than one clear role.

understand.visual_alt: Two word-pair columns showing universalism:particularism and diffuseness:specificity as antonym pairs.

given.aim: Find the word that stands in the same relation to diffuseness as particularism stands to universalism

terms.meaning: An antonym pair — broad universal application vs. narrow relational application
terms.connects: Establishes the type of relation: direct antonyms

terms.meaning: Being broadly spread, vague, or covering everything loosely
terms.example: Diffuse light spreads in all directions rather than focusing on one spot
terms.connects: First word of the second pair; needs its antonym

terms.meaning: Being precise, narrow, and exactly defined — the direct antonym of diffuseness
terms.example: A specific instruction names exactly one task to perform
terms.connects: Mirrors particularism's role as the narrow/precise counterpart

given.plan: Identify the relationship in the first pair (antonyms: broad vs. narrow scope), then find the word that forms the same antonymous relationship with diffuseness.

steps.title: Establish the relationship in the given pair
steps.apply: Universalism = applying rules broadly to all; Particularism = applying rules narrowly to specific cases. The relationship is: broad/general OPPOSITE narrow/specific.
steps.note: This is a direct antonym pair on the axis of scope (broad vs. narrow).

steps.title: Apply the same relationship to diffuseness
steps.apply: Diffuseness = broadly spread, vague, unfocused. Its antonym on the same axis = specificity (precise, narrow, exactly defined). Check options: B. neutrality (unrelated axis), C. generality (synonym of diffuseness, not antonym), D. adaptation (different axis entirely). Only A. specificity fits.
steps.note: Generality would be a near-synonym, not an antonym; neutrality and adaptation are off-axis entirely.

solution.result: A. specificity — diffuseness (broad/vague) is the antonym of specificity (precise/narrow), mirroring universalism vs. particularism.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas are involved; the question tests lexical analogy using antonymous word pairs.

questions.question:
Which one of the following combinations is incorrect?

A. Acquiescence - Submission  B. Wheedle - Roundabout  C. Flippancy - Lightness  D. Profligate - Extravagant
questions.to_find: the word pair in which the two words are NOT synonyms or do not share the same meaning

understand.plain: Each option presents two words intended to be synonyms or close in meaning. The task is to find the pair where the words are NOT synonymous. Three of the four pairs are valid synonym pairs; one is incorrect because the second word does not match the meaning of the first.

keywords.explain: Reluctant acceptance or agreement without protest; passive consent.
keywords.example: His acquiescence to the unfair terms showed he lacked confidence to argue.

keywords.explain: To persuade someone to do something by using flattery or coaxing; to cajole or sweet-talk.
keywords.example: She wheedled her father into lending her the car by complimenting him all morning.

keywords.explain: Treating serious matters with inappropriate lightness or casualness; lack of proper seriousness.
keywords.example: His flippancy during the funeral upset everyone present.

understand.visual_alt: A table of four word pairs, with the incorrect pair (Wheedle - Roundabout) highlighted.

given.aim: Identify the incorrectly paired synonyms among the four options

terms.meaning: Both mean passive acceptance or yielding; these are genuine synonyms
terms.example: Both words describe giving in without resistance
terms.connects: This pair is CORRECT — valid synonym pair

terms.meaning: Wheedle means to coax/flatter; Roundabout means indirect or circuitous — these are NOT synonyms
terms.example: Wheedling uses flattery to persuade; a roundabout route goes the long way
terms.connects: This pair is INCORRECT — the words have different meanings

terms.meaning: Both mean spending recklessly or wastefully; valid synonyms
terms.example: A profligate spender burns through money just as an extravagant one does
terms.connects: This pair is CORRECT — valid synonym pair

given.plan: Verify each word pair for synonymy; the pair where the second word does NOT match the meaning of the first is the answer.

steps.title: Verify each pair
steps.apply: A. Acquiescence (passive consent) - Submission (yielding) => CORRECT synonym pair. C. Flippancy (casual lightness) - Lightness (lack of seriousness) => CORRECT. D. Profligate (wastefully extravagant) - Extravagant (spending excessively) => CORRECT.
steps.note: Three of four pairs match on the same semantic dimension.

steps.title: Identify the incorrect pair
steps.apply: B. Wheedle means to coax or cajole using flattery. Roundabout means indirect, circuitous, or going around a topic. These two words belong to completely different semantic fields: Wheedle is about persuasion tactics, while Roundabout is about directness/path. They are NOT synonyms.
steps.note: The likely confusion is with 'roundabout ways of speaking' and persuasion, but Wheedle specifically means coaxing via flattery — not indirectness.

solution.result: B. Wheedle - Roundabout is the incorrect pair; Wheedle means to coax/flatter, not to be indirect.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas; the question tests vocabulary knowledge of synonym relationships.

questions.question:
Select the pair that best expresses a relationship similar to that expressed in the pair:

Children : Pediatrician

A. Adult : Orthopaedist  B. Females : Gynaecologist  C. Kidney : Nephrologist  D. Skin : Dermatologist
questions.to_find: the pair that shares the same relationship as Children : Pediatrician

understand.plain: A pediatrician is a doctor who specializes in treating children — that is, a specific group of people (patients defined by demographic). The analogy asks for another specialist-patient pair where the specialist is defined by the demographic group of their patients, not by a body part or organ.

keywords.explain: A doctor specializing in the medical care of children and infants.
keywords.example: Parents take their newborn to a pediatrician for vaccinations and growth checks.

keywords.explain: A doctor specializing in the health of the female reproductive system; their patient group is females.
keywords.example: A gynaecologist performs routine check-ups specific to women's reproductive health.

keywords.explain: A doctor specializing in kidney diseases — defined by the organ (kidney), not a demographic group of patients.
keywords.example: A patient with chronic kidney disease is referred to a nephrologist.

understand.visual_alt: A diagram showing patient-type to specialist links: Children->Pediatrician, Females->Gynaecologist as demographic-based pairs, versus Kidney->Nephrologist as organ-based pair.

given.aim: Find the pair where the specialist treats a demographic group (not an organ/body part), matching Children:Pediatrician

terms.meaning: A demographic group (age-defined patients) paired with their specialist doctor
terms.example: Children are the patient group; Pediatrician is their specialist
terms.connects: Template relationship: demographic patient group -> their specialist

terms.meaning: A demographic group (gender-defined patients) paired with their specialist doctor
terms.example: Females are the patient group; Gynaecologist is their specialist
terms.connects: Mirrors the template: demographic patient group -> their specialist

terms.meaning: Body parts or organs paired with their specialist — the relationship is organ-to-specialist, not demographic-to-specialist
terms.example: Kidney is not a patient group; it is a body part
terms.connects: Does NOT mirror the template; different relationship type

given.plan: Identify the relationship type in the given pair (demographic group -> their specialist doctor), then choose the option that uses the same relationship type rather than organ -> specialist.

steps.title: Classify the given pair's relationship
steps.apply: Children is a demographic group (defined by age). Pediatrician is a doctor who specializes in treating that demographic group. Relationship: (group of patients defined by who they are) -> (their specialist doctor).
steps.note: The key is that Children is a group of people, not an organ or disease.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. Adult:Orthopaedist — Orthopaedist treats bone/joint disorders in anyone, not specific to 'adults' as a demographic; the specialist is not defined by the adult demographic. C. Kidney:Nephrologist — Kidney is an organ, not a demographic group. D. Skin:Dermatologist — Skin is a body part, not a demographic group. B. Females:Gynaecologist — Females is a demographic group, and Gynaecologist specifically treats that group. MATCH.
steps.note: Options C and D are organ-to-specialist pairs; option A does not cleanly fit because orthopaedics is not restricted to adults; option B is the cleanest match.

solution.result: B. Females : Gynaecologist — Females is a demographic patient group, and a Gynaecologist is their specialist, mirroring Children:Pediatrician.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas; the question tests analogy reasoning based on word relationship types.

questions.question:
Select the word that fits the analogy:

Cook : Cook :: Fly : _______

A. Flyer  B. Flying  C. Flew  D. Flighter
questions.to_find: the word that completes the analogy Cook:Cook::Fly:___

understand.plain: In the pair 'Cook : Cook', the same word serves as both a verb and a noun. A cook (noun) is a person who cooks (verb). The analogy asks: what noun is formed from the verb 'fly' in the same way? A person who flies is called a Flyer. The pattern is verb-to-person-noun using the same or derived root.

keywords.explain: A word that functions as both a verb and a noun; 'cook' is a verb (action) and a noun (person who performs that action).
keywords.example: 'Cook' in 'I cook dinner' (verb) vs. 'She is a cook' (noun for person).

keywords.explain: A noun formed from a verb to describe the person who performs that action, often by adding '-er' or '-or' to the verb root.
keywords.example: 'Fly' -> 'Flyer' (a person who flies); 'Drive' -> 'Driver' (a person who drives).

keywords.explain: A noun meaning a person or thing that flies; the agent noun derived from the verb 'fly'.
keywords.example: A frequent flyer accumulates airline miles by flying often.

understand.visual_alt: A two-column analogy table: Cook (verb)/Cook (noun) matched to Fly (verb)/Flyer (noun).

given.aim: Find the noun form of 'fly' that represents the agent (person who flies), just as 'cook' is both verb and agent noun

terms.meaning: The action of preparing food by applying heat
terms.connects: First element of the analogy pair; establishes the verb

terms.meaning: A person who cooks; the agent noun from the verb cook — identical in form
terms.connects: Second element; shows that verb and agent noun are identical

terms.meaning: A person who flies; the agent noun derived from the verb 'fly' using the '-er' suffix
terms.example: He is an experienced flyer who travels for business.
terms.connects: Completes the analogy as the agent noun corresponding to the verb 'fly'

given.plan: In Cook:Cook, the same word is both verb and agent noun (person who performs the action). For Fly, the corresponding agent noun is Flyer (person who flies).

steps.title: Decode the relationship in Cook:Cook
steps.apply: The first 'Cook' = verb (to cook). The second 'Cook' = noun (a person who cooks). The relationship is: (verb) -> (agent noun / person who performs the verb). Here the verb and agent noun happen to be the same word.

steps.title: Find the agent noun for 'fly'
steps.apply: Fly (verb) -> agent noun = Flyer (a person who flies). Check options: B. Flying = present participle (not a person noun); C. Flew = past tense (not a noun); D. Flighter = not a real English word. A. Flyer = valid agent noun for fly.
steps.note: Flyer is the standard English agent noun for the verb fly.

solution.result: A. Flyer — the agent noun corresponding to 'fly' is 'flyer', just as the agent noun for 'cook' is 'cook'.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas; the question tests understanding of verb-to-agent-noun relationships in English.

questions.question:
___________ is to surgery as writer is to ___________

Which one of the following options maintains a similar logical relation in the above sentence?

A. Plan, outline  B. Hospital, library  C. Doctor, book  D. Medicine, grammar
questions.to_find: the pair of words that fills the blanks so the analogy holds: ___ is to surgery as writer is to ___

understand.plain: The analogy is of the form: X is to (their domain/tool) as writer is to (their domain/tool). A writer writes books — a book is the output or primary product of a writer. Surgery is the domain or primary work of a doctor. So the analogy is: Doctor is to surgery as writer is to book. Both Doctor:surgery and writer:book express the person-to-their-primary-work relationship.

keywords.explain: A medical professional whose primary professional activity (work domain) is surgery and medicine.
keywords.example: A doctor performs surgery just as a writer produces books.

keywords.explain: The primary product or output of a writer's work — what a writer creates.
keywords.example: An author's career is defined by the books they write.

keywords.explain: An analogy type where a person is paired with their primary professional domain, tool, or output.
keywords.example: Painter:canvas, Sculptor:statue, Singer:song all follow the same pattern.

understand.visual_alt: Two parallel analogy pairs: Doctor -> Surgery and Writer -> Book, both showing person-to-primary-work relationship.

given.aim: Find the person whose primary domain is surgery, and the object that is the primary product/domain of a writer

terms.meaning: Need a person (profession) whose primary work is surgery
terms.example: A doctor/surgeon performs surgery as their core professional activity
terms.connects: The blank must be a practitioner of surgery

terms.meaning: Need the primary output or domain of a writer
terms.example: A writer's primary output is a book
terms.connects: The blank must be what a writer produces or primarily works with

terms.meaning: Doctor is the person who performs surgery; book is the product of a writer — both pairs use person-to-primary-work relationship
terms.example: Doctor:surgery :: writer:book — both define someone by their primary professional work
terms.connects: Correctly fills both blanks with the same relationship type

given.plan: The relationship is (professional person) : (their primary work/output). Surgery -> Doctor; Writing -> Book. Verify other options do not hold this relationship.

steps.title: Identify the relationship type
steps.apply: ___ is to surgery = the person whose core work is surgery => Doctor (or Surgeon). Writer is to ___ = the core output/product of a writer => Book. The relationship is: professional person : their primary professional output or domain.
steps.note: Both halves of the analogy must use the same relationship type.

steps.title: Verify each option
steps.apply: A. Plan:outline — Plan is to surgery? No clear relationship; plan is not a person who performs surgery. B. Hospital:library — Hospital is the place surgery happens; library is the place books are kept. The relation shifts from person->work to place->resource. C. Doctor:book — Doctor performs surgery (person:work); writer writes book (person:work). MATCHES. D. Medicine:grammar — Medicine is related to surgery as a field, but writer:grammar is a loose linguistic tool rather than a writer's primary product.
steps.note: Option C is the only one where both blanks preserve the person-to-primary-work relationship.

solution.result: C. Doctor, book — Doctor is to surgery as writer is to book; both pairs express a professional person and their primary domain/output.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas; the question tests professional analogy reasoning (person:primary work relationship).

questions.question:
Pen : Write :: Knife : _______

(By word meaning)

Which one of the following options maintains a similar logical relation in the above?

A. Vegetables  B. Sharp  C. Cut  D. Blunt
questions.to_find: the word that stands in the same relation to Knife as Write stands to Pen

understand.plain: A pen's primary function is to write. The analogy asks for the primary function of a knife. A knife is used to cut. So the pattern is (tool) : (its primary function/action). Pen writes; a knife cuts. The answer is the verb that describes what a knife does.

keywords.explain: An analogy where a tool is paired with its primary function or the action it performs.
keywords.example: Hammer:Nail (what it drives in), Pen:Write (what it does), Knife:Cut (what it does).

keywords.explain: A pen is the instrument; Write is the action it performs. This establishes the template: instrument -> its action.
keywords.example: Using a pen to write a letter shows the tool-to-action relationship.

keywords.explain: A knife is the instrument; Cut is the action it performs. Mirrors Pen:Write exactly.
keywords.example: Using a knife to cut vegetables shows the same tool-to-action relationship.

understand.visual_alt: Two analogy pairs side by side: Pen -> Write (tool -> action) and Knife -> Cut (tool -> action).

given.aim: Find the primary action/function of a knife, matching the tool-to-function relationship of Pen:Write

terms.meaning: A writing instrument; a tool whose primary function is the action of writing
terms.example: A pen is used to write text on paper
terms.connects: Establishes the tool side of the analogy

terms.meaning: The primary function or action performed by a pen
terms.example: You write letters, words, and sentences using a pen
terms.connects: Establishes the function/action side of the analogy

terms.meaning: The primary function or action performed by a knife — to slice or divide
terms.example: A chef uses a knife to cut vegetables and meat
terms.connects: Completes the analogy as the action corresponding to the tool Knife

given.plan: The relationship is tool : primary-action. Pen -> Write; Knife -> Cut. Reject options that are not the primary action: Vegetables (object being cut, not the action), Sharp (an attribute of a knife, not an action), Blunt (the opposite attribute).

steps.title: Establish the analogy type
steps.apply: Pen : Write = (tool) : (the action the tool performs). A pen performs the action of writing. Therefore the blank for Knife must be the action the knife performs.
steps.note: The relationship is instrument-to-primary-action, not instrument-to-object or instrument-to-attribute.

steps.title: Identify the correct action for Knife and eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: A. Vegetables — vegetables are the object acted upon by a knife, not the action (analogous to 'paper' for a pen, not 'write'). B. Sharp — sharpness is an attribute of a knife, not an action. D. Blunt — also an attribute (opposite of sharp). C. Cut — to cut is the primary action performed by a knife. MATCHES the template.
steps.note: Keeping the relationship type consistent (tool : action) uniquely selects Cut.

solution.result: C. Cut — a knife's primary function is to cut, just as a pen's primary function is to write.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas; the question tests tool-to-function analogy reasoning in English vocabulary.
